answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's poem "Ek haji Madinay ke raastay mein" (from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, chapter 6), the pilgrim (haji) faces a dramatic ordeal on the road to Medina (Yathrib).**[^1]

The caravan is plundered by bandits in the desert, far from its goal, with no safe harbor in sight.[^2] Many fellow travelers are slain by dagger; the survivors, hearts broken, turn back toward the Kaaba (God's House in Mecca).[^3] Notably, a youth from Bukhara dies with joy, viewing the bandit's dagger as the Eid crescent moon and invoking "Ah, Yathrib!" on his lips as he embraces martyrdom.[^4]

The pilgrim grapples with fear warning against proceeding to Medina's gates, countered by passion urging him onward as a fearless Muslim.[^5] He questions abandoning the journey and facing shame before the Prophet on Judgment Day.[^6] Ultimately, the Hejaz wanderer defies death—echoing the Hijra's spirit—choosing the bliss of risky passion over the safe Syrian caravan, with bold soul triumphing over calculating reason.[^7]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 6 "Ek haji Madinay ke raastay mein" ([translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/06-ek-haji-madinay-ke-raastay-mein/translation/)).

[^5]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/06-ek-haji-madinay-ke-raastay-mein/translation/#p-f880d8]
